segments.s376-g57: فرسته is the direct object 'an envoy/messenger' placed before the verb (typical in Classical Persian prose/poetry). فرستاد is the past‑tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'sent'. با + خواسته ('with the requested/desired') begins an accompanying/instrumental phrase: خواسته here functions as adjective or noun meaning 'requested/desired (things/people)'.
segments.s376-g58: غلامان و اسپان is a coordinated noun phrase 'attendants (servants) and horses'; note the archaic plural اسپان (instead of modern اسبان). آراسته is a post‑positive adjective 'adorned/arrayed' modifying the whole coordinated phrase (adjectives commonly follow nouns in Classical Persian).
segments.s376-g59: شه بت‌پرستان is read as 'the idol‑worshipping shah' (شه 'king' with the descriptive genitive/compound بت‌پرستان 'idol‑worshippers'); رایان هند is the personal name 'Rayan of India' (name + place). The conjunction و links the two recipients; in Classical Persian such recipients/objects may appear before the verb.
segments.s376-g60: گزیتش is the proper name گزیت (Gazit) with the enclitic -ش (3rd‑person clitic marking the given thing); this marks Gazit as the thing given. بدادند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they gave' (note the prefixed بـ common in classical verse before past stems). شاهان سند 'the kings of Sind' is the subject placed after the verb (object(s) preposed, verb, then subject — a common Classical Persian ordering).
segments.s376-g25: هم‌آنگاه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at that moment' placed at the sentence start for temporal framing. بستور (an older spelling of به‌طور) is an adverb 'in that manner' modifying the following verb. برد here is a past-tense verb 'he took/led away' (classical Persian بردن); آن سپاه is the demonstrative + noun 'that army' and is the object; word order is somewhat flexible in poetry, so the verb appears before its object.
segments.s376-g26: و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. شاه جهان is a compound nominal phrase 'the king of the world' (شاه = king, جهان = world). از بر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'from off the throne' (از + بر + تخت; classical usage stacks prepositions), and و گاه adds 'and the seat/place' (گاه here is poetic for a place/seat). The whole phrase describes the king departing or rising from his throne/seat.
segments.s376-g27: نشست is the simple past 'he sat (down)'. و is 'and'. کیی is a title/epithet referring to the Kayanian dynasty (used here as the royal actor). تاج بر سر نهاد is a verb phrase 'placed the crown on the head' where تاج (crown) is the object, بر سر is the locative 'on the head', and نهاد is the past-tense verb 'placed/put'.
segments.s376-g28: سپه را is the marked object/topic 'the army' (را is the definite/object marker). همه یکسره is an adverbial phrase 'all, completely / all at once' modifying the verb. بار داد is a verbal phrase 'gave a load/tribute' where بار = 'load/burden/tribute' and داد is the past-tense 'gave'. The sequence means he bestowed (a load/tribute) on the army in full.
segments.s376-g29: در گنج 'in the treasure/treasury' gives the location. بگشاد is a past-form meaning 'he opened' (classical/petrified form of 'to open'). وز here functions poetically as a compressed conjunctional phrase (و ز = 'and from/with' or 'and by') linking to خواسته 'desire/wish' — the phrase means 'and by (his) will/desire'. This is an idiomatic poetic construction rather than a literal noun sense of وز.
segments.s376-g30: سپه را is again the marked object 'the army'. همه is 'all' or 'entire'. کرد is the past 'made/did' and takes آراسته (adorned) as its complement: 'made (them) adorned' = 'equipped/adorned the whole army'. Adjective complements commonly follow the verb in Persian.
segments.s376-g31: سران را is 'to the chiefs/leaders' with را marking them as topical/definite recipients. همه شهرها داد is 'he gave all the cities' (همه = all; شهرها = cities; داد = gave). نیز 'also/likewise' links this gift-giving to the previous distribution: it's an additional statement about what was given to leaders.
segments.s376-g32: کسی را here is topicalized with را and functions in a negative-existential construction: نماند 'there remained' with negative force means 'there remained no one'. ایچ ناداده چیز is a final noun phrase 'a thing not given' (ایچ = nothing / not-anything; ناداده = not given; چیز = thing). Put together it states there was no person left for whom there existed a thing not given — i.e., no one remained without receiving something.
segments.s376-g49: Poetic clause: شبان is used as a poetic plural for “nights” (here translated “nights”); شده is the past participle/auxiliary meaning “having become” or “was”; تیره‌مان is a possessive compound (“our dark/bleak [time]”); روز کرد is a compound predicate where کرد (= “made”) takes روز as its object: “made (into) day.” The whole unit says that what had been nights/darkness was turned into day.
segments.s376-g50: Direct transitive clause: کیان را is the definite object (“the Kians” marked by را). به هر جای is a prepositional phrase “in/to every place” (به + هر + جای). پیروز کرد uses کرد as a causative/performative verb meaning “made/caused to be victorious.” The order is Verb-final for the verbal phrase (object before verb) typical of classical Persian.
segments.s376-g51: Passive/causative phrasing: به نفرین (lit. “by/with a curse”) gives the instrument or condition; شد is the copular/‘become’ verb; ارجاسپ is the subject/name; ناآفرین is a predicate adjective meaning “accursed/ill‑omened.” Together: “Arjasp became accursed by a curse.”
segments.s376-g52: Formulaic comment: چنین است is a fixed phrase meaning “thus is” or “so is”; کار is the noun “work/deed/affair”; جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun “world‑creator” (i.e. God). The clause states a general saying about divine action: “Thus is the work of the Creator.”
segments.s376-g53: Temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو = “when.” پیروزی شاهتان is a genitive/possessive phrase “the victory of your king(s).” بشنوید is second‑person plural present/subjunctive (used here in the sense “when you hear / if you hear”), a common classical sequence after چو.
segments.s376-g54: Main imperative/command clause: گزیتی is the proper name object (Gazit). به آذر پرستان is the dative phrase “to the fire‑worshippers” (به + compound); دهید is 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive of دادن meaning “give.” The clause is a direct order following the temporal ‘when’ clause.
segments.s376-g55: Temporal clause with چو = “when.” آگاه شد is “became aware / learned” (شد as copular/verb of becoming) and قیصر is the subject “the Caesar.” آن شاه روم functions as the content that the Caesar learned about (“that king of Rome”); classical Persian often places the object/that‑phrase after the verb.
segments.s376-g56: Reported content introduced by که = “that.” فرخ شد means “became fortunate” (فرخ = fortunate, شد = became). آن شاه is repeated for emphasis (“that king”) and و links to ارجاسپ; شوم is an adjective meaning “ill‑omened / accursed” (here predicated of Arjasp). The clause reports what the Caesar heard: the king’s good fortune and Arjasp’s ill fate.
segments.s376-g41: زمینش (zamin+-ash) is a possessed noun 'its ground/earth' with the pronominal suffix -ش. بکردند is a past plural verb 'they made/caused' (classical/poetic form with the b- prefix). The prepositional phrase از زر پاک 'from/of pure gold' follows the verb and marks the material; overall: 'they made its ground out of pure gold.'
segments.s376-g42: This is a nominal/elliptical clause with the copula omitted (typical in Persian): همه هیزمش = 'all its firewood' (همه + هیزم with possessive -ش). The sequence عود و عنبرش خاک lists two predicative phrases: 'oud (agarwood)' and 'its amber' with خاک 'dust/ashes' as the predicative noun; understood verb 'was/consisted of' is omitted. Word order puts the possessor-suffixed noun before the predicate.
segments.s376-g43: همه کاخ را uses را to mark the definite object 'the whole palace' (همه = 'entire/whole' + کاخ). کار اندام is a compound meaning roughly 'a work of form/ornament' (کار = work; اندام = form/figure). کرد is the past-tense verb 'he made/caused', so the clause means 'he made the whole palace into a work of form/beauty.'
segments.s376-g44: پسش is an adverb 'after that/then.' خان گشتاسپیان نام کرد is a transitive sequence 'named (it/him) Khan Gushtaspian' with نام کرد 'he named' as the verb; the direct object (the person or thing being named) is understood from context rather than repeated here. Word order: adverb → name/title → verb.
segments.s376-g45: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate clause expressing what was to be done ('that' / 'so that'). بر در گنبدش is a locative phrase 'on the door of his dome' (بر = on; در = door; گنبدش = his dome), showing where the action of the subordinate clause should take place.
segments.s376-g46: بدادند is a past plural 'they gave/granted/made'; Persian uses the pattern 'X را Y کردن/دادن' to mean 'make X into Y' or 'grant X Y.' Here جاماسپ را (Jamasp + را) is the patient/object and موبدش 'his mobed' is the office/title given; together: 'they made Jamasp his mobed.' The verb is plural because the doers are implied (they performed the appointment).
segments.s376-g47: سوی مرزدارانش is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward his border-guards' (سوی = toward/to; مرزدارانش = his border-guards). نامه نوشت is a simple past clause 'he wrote a letter' (نامه = letter; نوشت = he wrote). The verb follows the object in typical Persian SOV order.
segments.s376-g48: که introduces reported content ('that'). ما را uses را as an object/emphasis marker ('us'). خداوند is the agent 'God/the Lord.' یافه is a participial/past form 'found/obtained' (here meaning 'granted' or 'bestowed'). نهشت is an archaic/poetic past-form verb; in this construction the clause conveys that God has brought about or granted something to 'us' (the literal words are compact/elliptical in classical Persian).
segments.s376-g33: کرا is an archaic relative/indirect-object word meaning “to whom” (or “who/whom” in earlier Persian). پادشاهی (kingship) + سزا (desert/what one deserves) form a noun phrase meaning “whoever deserved kingship.” The verb phrase here is compressed/archaic; the line says he gave the kingship to those deserving — the older word order puts the recipient (کرا) first and the thing given (پادشاهی) right after it.
segments.s376-g34: کرا again = “to whom.” پایه بایست is an older way to say “for whom a base/foundation ought to stand” (بایست = should/must). پایه نهاد uses نهاد (past of نهادن, “placed/laid”) meaning “he set/laid the foundation.” The clause literally reads: “to whom a foundation ought to be (پایه بایست), he placed a foundation.”
segments.s376-g35: چو at the start = temporal ‘when’. اندر is the preposition ‘in/within’, and کارشان = ‘their affairs/work’. خور here is an older/poetic element meaning something like ‘internal/domestic/household’ (an exact modern sense isn’t in the given list). داد is the past verb ‘gave/assigned,’ and ساز acts as ‘means/arrangement/provision’ (ساز here = ‘provision’ or ‘that which was made’). Together: “When he gave them charge of their internal affairs / provided for their needs….”
segments.s376-g36: سوی = ‘toward/to’. خانهاشان = ‘their khans/lords’ (خان plural + possessive). فرستاد is the simple past ‘sent,’ and باز here means ‘back/again.’ The clause states he dispatched them back toward their respective khans.
segments.s376-g37: خرامید is the past tense ‘he strode/pranced’ (a proud, showy walk). بر + گاه و باره is a poetic doubling meaning ‘upon that time/occasion’ (both گاه and باره mean ‘time/occasion’). ببست (past of بستن) here is ‘he closed/fastened’ or more generally ‘he made ready/took action’ on that occasion; in this context it idiomatically accompanies the entrance and settling of the ruler into the hall.
segments.s376-g38: به = ‘to/into’. کاخ شهنشاهی = ‘the imperial palace’ (کاخ = palace; شهنشاهی = imperial/royal). اندر = ‘within/inside,’ and نشست is the past of نشستن ‘he sat/settled.’ The clause simply gives the location: he sat in the imperial palace.
segments.s376-g39: بفرمود is an honorific past form ‘he commanded/ordered’ (polite third-person). تا introduces purpose/result here ‘so that/that,’ آذر is the poetic word for ‘fire,’ and افروختند is the past plural ‘they kindled/lit.’ Together: ‘He commanded that they kindle the fire.’
segments.s376-g40: برو here functions as a short narrative particle meaning roughly ‘then’ or indicating what follows (the literal listed senses ‘go’ do not capture this narrative use, so the exact sense is not in the provided list). عود و عنبر = ‘agarwood (oud) and ambergris’ — aromatic substances — همی is the continuous/emphatic particle (‘indeed/still’ used here as a simple narrative marker), and سوختند is the past plural ‘they burned.’ The clause reports that agarwood and ambergris were burned (for scent/incense).
segments.s376-g9: This is an SOV-style clause: object (همه خستگان + را) precedes the verb ببردند. را is the definite object marker attached to خستگان. ببردند is a past plural verb 'they took' (classical/past narrative), and نیز at the end is an enclitic adverb 'also' linking this action to previous events.
segments.s376-g10: نماندند is the past negative sense 'did not remain / there was not left' (here used impersonally about things). از introduces the source/partitive 'of/from', خواسته is a noun 'that which was sought / desired', and the clause-final نیز چیز ('even a thing') emphasizes that not even one item remained; word order is marked and poetic, with modifiers fronted for emphasis.
segments.s376-g11: به ایران‌زمین is a prepositional phrase 'to the land of Iran' (به = to). باز is an adverb 'again' placed before the verb for emphasis. بردندشان is the past plural 'they brought/took them' with the clitic شان 'them' attached to the verb, showing the object is pronominal.
segments.s376-g12: Another prepositional phrase (به + recipient). دانا is an adjective 'wise' modifying پزشکان 'physicians'. سپردندشان is the past plural verb 'they entrusted/handed them over' with the clitic -شان again marking 'them' as object; the order (PP + verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s376-g13: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. شاه جهان is a title 'king of the world' (noun + apposition). باز شد is a past verbal phrase 'became again / recovered' (باز = again, شد = became/was). بازجای is a fused adverbial/nominal phrase 'again in place / back to his post' completing the idea of restoration.
segments.s376-g14: This clause places the recipient phrase before the verb and ends with the agent's name: به پور مهین = 'to Pour‑Mahin' (به = to; پور مهین = name/epithet), داد is the past verb 'gave', and فرخ همای is the subject (a proper name) appearing after the verb, a permitted poetic order in classical Persian.
segments.s376-g15: سپه را is the marked object 'the army' (را = object marker). به بستور plays as a manner phrase 'in this way' (به + بستور). فرخنده modifies the giving as 'auspicious/fortunate', and داد is the past verb 'gave/bestowed' — together, they describe how the army was handed over. Word order (object + manner phrase + modifier + verb) is conventional in poetic narrative.
segments.s376-g16: This is a general statement: عجم را marks 'the Persians' as topic. چنین بود is 'was thus' (چنین = thus, بود = was, past). آیین و داد are the nominal predicates 'custom and justice', giving a summary statement about Persian practice; the order (topic + predicate) is customary in older Persian.
segments.s376-g17: بدادش here functions as the verb phrase 'he gave him' in the narrative (a literary past action). The preposition از ('from') introduces the source group (آزادگان, 'the free/nobles') and the numeral phrase 'ده هزار' ('ten thousand') follows the source to indicate quantity. Word order is: (verb + clitic object) + prepositional source + numeral phrase + noun.
segments.s376-g18: This is a noun phrase describing the force given: سواران ('horsemen') modified by جنگی (a warlike/military adjective), then the coordinating و ('and') links a compound role formed with نیزه + گزار = 'spear-bearers' (نیزه گزار). The adjectives follow the nouns in this poetic register, and گزار here functions as a second element forming a compound 'spear-bearer' rather than its literal sense 'narrator'.
segments.s376-g19: بفرمود و گفت is a doubled reporting formula common in epic Persian: both verbs indicate a single speech act ('he commanded/said'). ای is the vocative marker 'O!'. گو رزمسار here addresses the addressee as 'O warrior' (گو functions as part of the vocative/title in this line), so the whole segment introduces the direct command that follows.
segments.s376-g20: This is the command clause: یکی ('one' / 'someone') is the indefinite subject; بر پی (here 'بر' + 'پی') means 'upon/in pursuit of' (on the track of), شاه توران = 'the king of Turan', and بتاز is an imperative 'charge/ride hard'. The order places the indefinite subject first, then the directional phrase, then the object (شاه توران) and ends with the verb-imperative بتاز.
segments.s376-g21: به marks the direction/target 'to/through' and introduces place names (ایتاش and خلخ ستان = 'Aitash and Khalkhestan'). و simply coordinates the two place-names. برگذر is an imperative/infinitival form meaning 'let pass / pass by' (here an order to pass those regions). The sequence is a short command telling the rider which route to take.
segments.s376-g22: This is a second command: بکش is the imperative 'kill!'; هرک + یابی forms an indefinite relative 'whoever you find' (هرک 'whoever' + یابی, here subjunctive/relative form of 'to find'); به کین پدر is a purpose/justification phrase 'for the vengeance of (his) father' or 'to avenge the father'. The clause is an imperative with an indefinite object defined by a subjunctive-like relative.
segments.s376-g23: ز (poetic 'from') introduces the domain 'هرچیز' ('everything' / 'anything'). بایست is a modal 'must/ought' (here third-person impersonal/literary use: 'he should'), and بردش is the verb phrase 'take/use it' (بردن + object clitic -ش) with meaning 'employ/take (for) use' in this context; the particle به کار ('to/for use') follows to mark purpose — together: 'from everything he must take (what is) of use.'
segments.s376-g24: بدادش here continues the narratorial action 'he gave him' (a past action), followed by همه 'all' and two adjectives joined by و: بی‌مر (an archaic/uncertain adjective here functioning as an intensive modifier) and بی‌شمار 'countless'. The sequence lists the extent of the gift: 'he gave him all, innumerable.'
segments.s376-g1: کی here is the temporal adverb 'when' introducing the clause. "نامبردار فرخنده شاه" is a noun phrase of stacked epithets: نامبردار (renowned) and فرخنده (auspicious/fortunate) both modify شاه (king). Classical word order allows modifiers before the head noun in such epic diction.
segments.s376-g2: سوی is the preposition 'toward/to' marking direction; گاه functions here as the destination ('the court/place' in archaic/poetic sense); باز آمد is the past action 'returned' with باز as the adverb 'again' placed before آمد for emphasis; از رزمگاه marks the source 'from the battlefield.'
segments.s376-g3: به بستور is an idiomatic phrase 'in this manner / thus'; گفتا is the archaic past-tense 'he said' (classical Shahnameh diction). که introduces the reported speech. فردا is the temporal adverb 'tomorrow.' پکاه is an epithet or vocative ('pure/untainted' used to address someone) inside the reported order.
segments.s376-g4: سوی is again the preposition 'toward'; کشور نامور is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned country/realm' (نامور modifies کشور). کش here is the verb 'draw/lead' (an imperative or hortative inside reported speech), and سپاه is the direct object 'host/army.' Classical word order places the direction phrase before the verb + object.
segments.s376-g5: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (ancient narrative form); سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (commander); هم 'also' links this subject to previous action; از بامداد is a temporal phrase 'from dawn / in the morning.' Note the verb precedes the nominal subject, a common feature in epic Persian.
segments.s376-g6: بزد is the past tense 'he struck/beat' (here used with کوس to mean 'beat the war‑drum' as a signal); و is the conjunction 'and'; لشکر is 'army/host'; بنه here is a noun that in this context goes with برنهاد to mean 'set up/array' (poetic phrasing), and برنهاد is the past 'placed/raised' — together indicating that he marshalled or put the host in order.
segments.s376-g7: به ایران‌زمین marks the direction 'toward the land of Iran'; باز is the adverb 'again' or 'back'; کردند is the past plural 'they made/did' (here used with روی as the verb phrase 'they turned [their] faces'); روی functions as 'face' and is the element being 'turned' — together forming 'they turned their faces back to Iran.'
segments.s376-g8: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; خیره دل گشته is a nominal predicate: خیره modifies دل ('hearts become astonished') and گشته is the perfect participle 'have become' — so 'all became astonished‑hearted.' و جنگجوی adds a coordinating adjective/noun 'and warlike/warriors,' describing their newly martial disposition. This compact adjectival/nominal stacking is typical in Ferdowsi's style.

sentences.s376-s29: He sent an envoy with the requested attendants and adorned horses.
sentences.s376-s30: The kings of Sind gave Gazit to the idol‑worshipping shah and to Rayan of India.
sentences.s376-s13: At that moment, in that manner he took that army, and the king of the world left the throne and the seat.
sentences.s376-s14: He sat down, and the Kayanian put the crown on his head; to the army he gave everything at once.
sentences.s376-s15: He opened the treasury, and, according to his wish, made the whole army adorned.
sentences.s376-s16: He gave cities to all the chiefs; likewise, there remained no one to whom any thing had not been given.
sentences.s376-s25: He turned our dark nights into day and made the Kians victorious everywhere.
sentences.s376-s26: Arjasp became accursed by the curse; such is the work of the World‑Creator.
sentences.s376-s27: When you hear of your king’s victory, give Gazit to the fire‑worshippers.
sentences.s376-s28: When the Caesar learned that that king of Rome had become fortunate and Arjasp accursed.
sentences.s376-s21: They made its ground of pure gold; all its firewood was oud, and its amber was dust.
sentences.s376-s22: He made the whole palace a work of form/beauty, and afterwards he named him Khan of Gushtasp.
sentences.s376-s23: He ordered that, on the door of his dome, they appoint Jamasp as his mobed (priest).
sentences.s376-s24: He wrote a letter to his border-guards, saying that the Lord had found/granted us (favor).
sentences.s376-s17: He gave the kingship to whoever deserved it; to whoever a foundation ought to be set, he laid a firm foundation.
sentences.s376-s18: When he entrusted them with their internal affairs, he provided for them and sent them back toward their khans.
sentences.s376-s19: He strode proudly to the court and, on the occasion, took his place; he sat within the imperial palace.
sentences.s376-s20: He ordered that they kindle the fire; then agarwood and ambergris were burned.
sentences.s376-s5: They also took away all the weary; not even a thing of what was sought remained.
sentences.s376-s6: They brought them back to the land of Iran and entrusted them to wise physicians.
sentences.s376-s7: When the king of the world recovered, Farrokh‑Homay gave the kingship back to Pour‑Mahin.
sentences.s376-s8: He bestowed the army in this auspicious manner; such was the custom and justice of the Persians.
sentences.s376-s9: He gave him ten thousand horsemen from the free — war-riders and spear-bearers.
sentences.s376-s10: He ordered and said, 'O warrior, let one ride after the king of Turan and charge!'
sentences.s376-s11: Pass by Aitash and Khalkhestan; kill whoever you find, for the vengeance of his father.
sentences.s376-s12: From everything he should take what is useful; he gave him all, innumerable and countless.
sentences.s376-s1: When the renowned, auspicious king returned to the court from the battlefield.
sentences.s376-s2: Thus he said: 'Tomorrow, O pure one, draw the famed host toward the realm.'
sentences.s376-s3: The spahbed also came at dawn; he struck the war‑drum and set the host in motion.
sentences.s376-s4: They turned their faces back toward the land of Iran; all became astonished‑hearted and warlike.
